evidence_text: Vitamin C reduces dietary ferric iron to the more soluble ferrous form, markedly enhancing non-heme iron absorption; pairing iron with a vitamin C source improves uptake, though strict co-timing is not mandatory.
Vitamin C regenerates the antioxidant form of alpha-tocopherol from its oxidized tocopheroxyl radical, recycling vitamin E activity.
Vitamin C is an essential cofactor for prolyl/lysyl hydroxylases in collagen synthesis; co-ingesting collagen peptides with vitamin C supports collagen formation.
Vitamin C is an essential cofactor for prolyl/lysyl hydroxylases in collagen synthesis; co-ingesting collagen peptides with vitamin C supports collagen formation.

Calcium competes with iron for absorption and forms poorly absorbed complexes; experts recommend taking calcium and iron at different times.

High calcium intake has been reported to reduce net zinc absorption or zinc balance in some human studies, although dietary-matrix effects are mixed; spacing calcium-rich meals away from zinc supplementation is reasonable when zinc absorption is a priority.

Calcium binds thyroid hormone (levothyroxine and desiccated thyroid) in the gut and markedly reduces absorption, risking treatment failure; guidelines require at least a 4-hour separation.

Caffeine antagonizes adenosine receptors and opposes melatonin's sleep-promoting action, blunting the effect of melatonin taken close to caffeine.

Dietary fat has been shown to improve quercetin bioavailability, likely by enhancing micellarization and intestinal absorption; taking quercetin with a fat-containing meal may improve absorption.

EPA/DHA absorption from omega-3 supplements can be improved when co-ingested with a fat-containing meal, especially for ethyl-ester forms; the effect depends on formulation, so this is best modeled as a soft meal-fat preference.

Thyroid hormone must be taken on an empty stomach; protein-rich food significantly delays and reduces its absorption, so dosing is separated from meals.
Thyroid hormone must be taken on an empty stomach; protein-rich food significantly delays and reduces its absorption, so dosing is separated from meals.
Dietary protein yields large neutral amino acids that competitively block levodopa transport across the gut and blood-brain barrier, reducing its effect; levodopa is dosed away from protein meals.
Supplemental L-tyrosine competes with dietary large neutral amino acids from a protein meal for the same transporters, reducing its brain uptake.
Supplemental L-tyrosine competes with dietary large neutral amino acids from a protein meal for the same transporters, reducing its brain uptake.
A protein-rich meal raises competing large neutral amino acids that limit tryptophan transport into the brain, blunting its serotonergic effect.
Dietary fiber reduces thyroid hormone (levothyroxine/desiccated thyroid) absorption; because this is narrow-therapeutic-index replacement therapy, the dose is separated from high-fiber meals.
Dietary fiber reduces thyroid hormone (levothyroxine/desiccated thyroid) absorption; because this is narrow-therapeutic-index replacement therapy, the dose is separated from high-fiber meals.
Phytate (phytic acid) is one of the most potent inhibitors of non-heme iron absorption, forming insoluble complexes in the gut.

Grapefruit furanocoumarins irreversibly inhibit intestinal CYP3A4, sharply raising levels of CYP3A4 substrates such as sirolimus toward toxic concentrations; the combination is avoided.
Red yeast rice contains monacolin K (lovastatin); grapefruit CYP3A4 inhibition can raise its levels several-fold and increase myopathy risk, so co-intake is avoided.
Grapefruit raises atorvastatin exposure by roughly 80% via CYP3A4 inhibition, increasing muscle-toxicity risk; avoidance is advised.
Trazodone is a CYP3A4 substrate; grapefruit can raise its plasma levels and sedative effect, so concurrent intake is discouraged.

Oxalate can complex iron in the gut and modestly reduce non-heme iron absorption.

Myrosinase hydrolyzes the glucosinolates in cruciferous food into active isothiocyanates such as sulforaphane; taking myrosinase with the food increases sulforaphane yield.

Acarbose should be taken with the first bite of each main meal; its glucose-lowering effect depends on being present with meal carbohydrates in the gut.
Berberine is taken just before or with a carbohydrate meal to blunt the post-meal glucose rise.

Metformin is recommended with or just after a meal to reduce GI side effects and target post-meal glucose.

Alcohol and metformin both raise blood lactate and alcohol blocks lactate clearance, increasing the risk of potentially fatal lactic acidosis; excessive alcohol is contraindicated.
Alcohol can increase the risk of stomach bleeding caused by aspirin; classify as a hard avoid rule for aspirin-containing regimens.
Alcohol increases trazodone nervous-system side effects such as dizziness, drowsiness, and impaired judgment; classify as a hard avoid rule.
Alcohol increases pregabalin nervous-system side effects such as dizziness, drowsiness, and impaired judgment; classify as a hard avoid rule.
Alcohol may reduce melatonin sleep effects and is best avoided during melatonin therapy; classify as a hard special-context avoid rule.

Caffeine paired with L-theanine is a well-characterized synergy: L-theanine smooths the stimulant curve, supporting attention while attenuating jitteriness. [SECONDARY SOURCE - verify primary RCT]

Adding dietary caffeine to other stimulant/methylxanthine ingredients produces additive CNS and cardiovascular stimulation (tachycardia, anxiety, disrupted sleep); total daily stimulant load should be considered.

Grapefruit furanocoumarins inhibit intestinal CYP3A4 and P-glycoprotein, pathways that also handle berberine; co-ingestion can raise berberine exposure and GI effects. Kept soft as berberine is not a narrow-therapeutic-index agent. [verify primary]

Grapefruit furanocoumarins inhibit intestinal CYP3A4 and P-glycoprotein, pathways that also handle berberine; co-ingestion can raise berberine exposure and GI effects. Kept soft as berberine is not a narrow-therapeutic-index agent. [verify primary]

Cruciferous glucosinolates are the dietary precursors converted via myrosinase to sulforaphane and related isothiocyanates; a glucosinolate-rich food augments precursor/enzyme supply for these precursor-type ingredients. Not intended for finished, stabilized sulforaphane products.

Cruciferous glucosinolates are the dietary precursors converted via myrosinase to sulforaphane and related isothiocyanates; a glucosinolate-rich food augments precursor/enzyme supply for these precursor-type ingredients. Not intended for finished, stabilized sulforaphane products.

High habitual cruciferous intake has mild goitrogenic potential (thiocyanate competes with iodide uptake) that can marginally oppose thyroid-hormone therapy; relevant mainly with high intake, low iodine status, or thyroid disease.

High habitual cruciferous intake has mild goitrogenic potential (thiocyanate competes with iodide uptake) that can marginally oppose thyroid-hormone therapy; relevant mainly with high intake, low iodine status, or thyroid disease.
EGCG strongly chelates non-heme iron in the gut and markedly inhibits its absorption; iron and green-tea catechins should be separated.

EGCG can act as a folate antagonist (dihydrofolate reductase inhibition), potentially lowering folate bioavailability at high catechin intake; treat as a low-weight caution. [SECONDARY SOURCE - verify primary]

Dietary nitrate feeds the nitrate-nitrite-NO pathway and acts synergistically with arginine/citrulline NO precursors to support vasodilation and exercise performance. [SECONDARY SOURCE - verify primary]

Dietary nitrate feeds the nitrate-nitrite-NO pathway and acts synergistically with arginine/citrulline NO precursors to support vasodilation and exercise performance. [SECONDARY SOURCE - verify primary]

Tyramine is a sympathomimetic pressor amine; on top of dopaminergic / aromatic-amino-acid precursors it adds a mild catecholaminergic pressor stimulus. Low clinical risk without an MAO inhibitor present.

Tyramine is a sympathomimetic pressor amine; on top of dopaminergic / aromatic-amino-acid precursors it adds a mild catecholaminergic pressor stimulus. Low clinical risk without an MAO inhibitor present.

Rhodiola shows weak in-vitro monoamine-oxidase-inhibiting activity; with a high-tyramine food this is a theoretical additive pressor concern, with low clinical risk at supplement doses.

Methylene blue is a potent monoamine-oxidase inhibitor; combined with a tyramine-rich aged/fermented food it blocks tyramine breakdown and can precipitate a hypertensive crisis. This is a hard contraindication-level timing constraint.

Concentrated or garlic-rich allium organosulfur compounds may inhibit platelet aggregation. When combined with aspirin, this may add bleeding-risk concern, so the food-source context is treated as a negative soft penalty rather than a strict prohibition.

Concentrated/garlic-rich allium organosulfur compounds have antiplatelet activity; stacked with other antiplatelet-leaning ingredients this is additive and mildly raises bleeding tendency.

Ginger constituents (gingerols/shogaols) have mild antiplatelet activity; combined with antiplatelet/anticoagulant-leaning ingredients the effect is additive. Kept soft (effect is modest and evidence weaker than garlic/curcumin).

Ginger has modest glucose-lowering activity; layered onto glucose-lowering agents it can additively reduce blood glucose. Evidence is effect-stacking (pharmacodynamic), not an absorption interaction.

High-dose turmeric or curcumin may inhibit platelet aggregation. When combined with aspirin or fibrinolytic agents such as nattokinase, this may add bleeding-risk concern, so the food-source context is treated as a negative soft penalty rather than a strict prohibition.

High-dose turmeric or curcumin may inhibit platelet aggregation. When combined with aspirin or fibrinolytic agents such as nattokinase, this may add bleeding-risk concern, so the food-source context is treated as a negative soft penalty rather than a strict prohibition.
High-dose turmeric/curcumin has antiplatelet activity; stacked with other antiplatelet-leaning ingredients this is additive and mildly raises bleeding tendency.
Curcumin is an iron chelator and can bind dietary/supplemental iron, reducing absorption and, with heavy intake, lowering iron stores.

A strongly acidic load can reduce the viability of acid-sensitive lactic-acid-bacteria and bifidobacteria probiotic strains; spacing protects viable counts. Applies only to acid-sensitive strains, not spore-formers/yeasts. [SECONDARY SOURCE - verify primary]

Organic acids in vinegar lower gastric pH and form soluble iron complexes, modestly enhancing non-heme iron absorption with the meal. Evidence is weaker than vitamin C -> iron.
Vinegar blunts post-meal glycemia; combined with glucose-lowering agents the effect is additive, a mild hypoglycemia caution (not a hard constraint). [SECONDARY SOURCE - verify primary]
